params.text:
The cycle of escalation

Conflict rarely jumps straight to crisis. It usually builds through recognisable stages: a person starts calm, then becomes anxious or agitated, then may reach a peak of anger or aggression, before gradually calming down and recovering. Each stage is an opportunity to step in and bring the tension back down.

The earlier you notice the signs and respond, the easier the situation is to defuse. Once someone reaches crisis point, safety becomes the priority and de-escalation is much harder. After the peak, people need time and calm to recover — pushing too soon can trigger a second escalation.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Early warning signs that tension is rising. Tap each card to see what to watch for.

dialogs.answer: Raised or louder voice, faster or sharper speech, sarcasm, swearing, repeated questions or demands, and challenging or threatening statements.
Clenched fists or jaw, flushed face, pacing, invading personal space, staring or glaring, tense posture, and restless or sudden movements.
Visible frustration, anxiety, fear, or feeling ignored. Anger and stress narrow a person's attention and reduce their ability to reason.
The sooner you recognise rising agitation, the more options you have to calm things down. Early action prevents escalation to crisis.
